near_misses.feedback: Sulfasalazine contains sulfapyridine (not a sulfone) and is used for IBD/RA. Trimethoprim acts on different folate pathway step. This drug is a sulfone antibiotic unique in its dual use for leprosy and dermatitis herpetiformis, with characteristic haemolytic toxicity in G6PD deficiency.

clues.label: Clinical Context
clues.text: This medication has an unusual dual identity — it is both an essential component of multidrug therapy for a chronic mycobacterial infection and the drug of choice for a blistering skin condition associated with coeliac disease.

clues.label: Pharmacology Clue
clues.text: It is a sulfone antibiotic that inhibits dihydropteroate synthase (part of the folate synthesis pathway) in susceptible organisms. Its anti-inflammatory mechanism in skin disease is less well understood but involves inhibition of neutrophil myeloperoxidase and suppression of neutrophil-mediated tissue damage.

clues.label: Key Feature
clues.text: The most important adverse effect is dose-related haemolysis, which occurs to some degree in all patients but is dramatically worse in those with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency. G6PD testing is mandatory before initiation.

clues.label: Narrowing Detail
clues.text: It can also cause methaemoglobinaemia (dose-related) and a rare but serious hypersensitivity syndrome presenting with fever, rash, hepatitis, and lymphadenopathy typically 2–6 weeks after starting treatment. Regular FBC and liver function monitoring is required.

clues.label: Identifying Detail
clues.text: For the mycobacterial infection, it is combined with rifampicin and clofazimine in the WHO-recommended multidrug regimen. For the blistering skin condition (dermatitis herpetiformis), it produces rapid improvement in pruritus within 24–48 hours, serving as both diagnostic confirmation and treatment.

puzzles.explanation: Dapsone is uniquely identified by its sulfone structure, dual role in leprosy (with rifampicin/clofazimine) and dermatitis herpetiformis, mandatory G6PD testing, dose-related haemolysis and methaemoglobinaemia, and the characteristic rapid response in dermatitis herpetiformis that serves as diagnostic confirmation.

The breadth of clues — spanning therapeutic use, mechanism, and unique characteristics — required integrating knowledge across multiple domains. near_misses.feedback: Ciclosporin also inhibits calcineurin but binds cyclophilin (not FKBP-12), has different side effects (gingival hyperplasia, hirsutism vs hair loss, diabetes), and different drug monitoring targets. This drug binds FKBP-12 and is the more commonly used calcineurin inhibitor in modern transplant protocols.

clues.label: Clinical Context
clues.text: This immunosuppressant is the backbone of modern solid organ transplant rejection prophylaxis, used in kidney, liver, heart, and lung transplantation. It is also used for severe atopic dermatitis (topical formulation) and refractory autoimmune conditions.

clues.label: Key Feature
clues.text: It inhibits calcineurin, a phosphatase enzyme required for T-cell activation, by forming a complex with an intracellular immunophilin. This blocks the nuclear translocation of nuclear factor of activated T-cells (NFAT) and suppresses interleukin-2 transcription.

clues.label: Pharmacology Clue
clues.text: It is primarily metabolised by CYP3A4 and is a substrate of P-glycoprotein, leading to numerous drug interactions. It has a narrow therapeutic index requiring regular therapeutic drug monitoring of whole-blood trough concentrations, with targets varying by organ transplanted and time post-transplant.

clues.label: Narrowing Detail
clues.text: Key adverse effects include nephrotoxicity (both acute and chronic), new-onset diabetes mellitus after transplantation (NODAT), neurotoxicity (tremor, headache, posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome), hyperkalaemia, and hypomagnesaemia. Alopecia distinguishes it from its older counterpart in the same class.

clues.label: Identifying Detail
clues.text: Originally isolated from the soil fungus Streptomyces tsukubaensis in Tsukuba, Japan, this macrolide lactone binds to FK-binding protein 12 (FKBP-12) — distinguishing it from the other calcineurin inhibitor which binds cyclophilin. It is approximately 100 times more potent than its predecessor.

puzzles.explanation: Tacrolimus is distinguished from ciclosporin by its FKBP-12 binding (vs cyclophilin), its association with diabetes and alopecia (vs gingival hyperplasia and hirsutism), 100-fold greater potency, and its origin from Streptomyces tsukubaensis. It is now the dominant calcineurin inhibitor in transplant medicine.
puzzles.walkthrough: Tacrolimus is a fascinating drug to identify through progressive reasoning. Starting from its broad therapeutic area, the key was recognizing the specific features that set it apart from similar agents. The pharmacological mechanism narrowed the field considerably, and the distinctive clinical details — particularly the identifying characteristics mentioned in the final clues — made it unmistakable. It is now the dominant calcineurin inhibitor in transplant medicine.

near_misses.feedback: Fluorouracil is an antimetabolite, diclofenac gel is an NSAID for actinic keratoses, and ingenol mebutate was withdrawn. This drug is unique in its immune-modulating mechanism via toll-like receptor 7 activation for skin lesions.

clues.label: Clinical Context
clues.text: This topical cream is used for several skin conditions including superficial basal cell carcinomas (when surgery is not appropriate), actinic (solar) keratoses, and external genital and perianal warts (condylomata acuminata).

clues.label: Key Feature
clues.text: Unlike other topical treatments for skin cancers that directly kill cells, this drug works by stimulating the patient's own immune system to attack abnormal cells. It has no direct antiviral or antitumour activity.

clues.label: Pharmacology Clue
clues.text: It is an immune response modifier that activates toll-like receptor 7 (TLR7) on dendritic cells and macrophages, stimulating production of interferon-alpha, tumour necrosis factor, and other pro-inflammatory cytokines that mount a localised immune response against the target lesion.

clues.label: Narrowing Detail
clues.text: Application causes a characteristic local inflammatory reaction — erythema, erosion, crusting, and sometimes flu-like symptoms. This reaction is expected and indicates the immune response is working. Patients should be counselled that the treated area will look worse before it looks better.

clues.label: Identifying Detail
clues.text: Applied as a 5% cream three times per week (for genital warts) or five times per week (for superficial BCC) for several weeks. It must be washed off after 6–10 hours. It is not suitable for internal warts, invasive BCC, or immunosuppressed patients. The expected inflammatory response distinguishes it from simple topical treatments.

puzzles.explanation: Imiquimod is the only topical TLR7 agonist in clinical use, uniquely identified by its immune-modulating mechanism (rather than direct cytotoxicity), the expected inflammatory skin reaction, and its dual indications across both viral (genital warts) and neoplastic (BCC, actinic keratoses) skin conditions.
